title: EdgeRelations
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG.

  1. If $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ then we call $v_1$ and $v_2$ adjacent in $G$.
  2. Edges of the form $v_1 \hut v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called into $v_1$.
    Edges of the form $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called into $v_2$.
  3. Edges of the form $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_2 \hut v_1$ are called out of $v_1$.

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg, and recall that $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ and $L \subseteq V \times V$, with $L$ symmetric in its two coordinates.

  1. Adjacency. For $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$, the nodes $v_1$ and $v_2$ are called adjacent in $G$ iff \[ (v_1, v_2) \in E \;\lor\; (v_2, v_1) \in E \;\lor\; (v_1, v_2) \in L, \] equivalently iff $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ (def not-cdmg, item 7).
  2. Edge into a vertex. The relations into and out of below classify edges of $G$ (the underlying ordered pairs of $E$ or of $L$) relative to a single bound vertex parameter $v$ – not relative to a particular labelling $v_1, v_2$ of the edge's endpoints. For an ordered pair $e = (e_1, e_2) \in (J \cup V) \times (J \cup V)$ and a vertex $v \in J \cup V$, we call $e$ an edge into $v$ (in $G$) iff \[ \bigl(e \in E \;\land\; e_2 = v\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(e \in L \;\land\; (e_1 = v \,\lor\, e_2 = v)\bigr). \] In particular, only ordered pairs $e \in E \cup L$ can be edges into $v$.
  3. Edge out of a vertex. For an ordered pair $e = (e_1, e_2) \in (J \cup V) \times (J \cup V)$ and a vertex $v \in J \cup V$, we call $e$ an edge out of $v$ (in $G$) iff \[ e \in E \;\land\; e_1 = v. \] In particular, no $L$-edge is out of any vertex.
  4. Reconciliation with the source-block writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$, $v_1 \hut v_2$, $v_1 \huh v_2$. The two writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ both abbreviate the single underlying statement $(v_1, v_2) \in E$ (def not-cdmg, items 2–3); and the two writings $v_1 \huh v_2$ and $v_2 \huh v_1$ both abbreviate $(v_1, v_2) \in L$, which moreover coincides with $(v_2, v_1) \in L$ by the symmetry of $L$ (def def-cdmg). Hence membership of an edge in the categories into $v$ or out of $v$ depends only on the underlying element of $E$ or of $L$, not on which of these textual writings is used; in particular, the source block's listing $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_2 \hut v_1$ in its item 3 and its single-writing listings in item 2 are equivalent under this identification.

    Spelled out per writing, items ii.–iii.\ above agree with the source block's items 2–3 as follows.

    • An $E$-edge written $v_1 \tuh v_2$ – i.e.\ underlying ordered pair $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, equivalently written $v_2 \hut v_1$ – is into $v_2$ (clause ii.\ fires via $e \in E$ with $e_2 = v_2$) and out of $v_1$ (clause iii.\ fires via $e \in E$ with $e_1 = v_1$).
    • An $E$-edge written $v_1 \hut v_2$ – i.e.\ underlying ordered pair $(v_2, v_1) \in E$, equivalently written $v_2 \tuh v_1$ – is into $v_1$ and out of $v_2$, by the same clauses with $v_1$ and $v_2$ interchanged.
    • An $L$-edge written $v_1 \huh v_2$ – i.e.\ underlying ordered pair $(v_1, v_2) \in L$, equivalently $(v_2, v_1) \in L$ and equivalently written $v_2 \huh v_1$ – is into $v_1$ and into $v_2$ (the $L$-clause of ii.\ fires for either endpoint), and is out of neither $v_1$ nor $v_2$ (clause iii.\ requires $e \in E$, whereas $e \in L$).

    In particular, into $v$ and out of $v$ are not complementary classifications of edges incident at $v$: an $L$-edge incident at $v$ is into $v$ but never out of $v$.

lean_blocks.explanation: Section-level `variable` declaration that fixes the ambient node type `Node` for the rest of the file. The unbounded universe `Type*` lets `Node` live in any universe, matching the polymorphism style used throughout `CDMG.lean` and `CDMGNotation.lean`. The `[DecidableEq Node]` instance is required because `CDMG.E` and `CDMG.L` are `Finset (Node × Node)`, and Mathlib's `Finset` operations (membership, intersection, decidable equality lifted to pairs) need `DecidableEq` on the underlying carrier. Once declared with `variable`, every subsequent `def adjacent`, `def into`, `def outOf` in this file silently picks up `{Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments, so the signatures stay terse and uniform.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Polymorphic node carrier; `Type*` allows any universe.
-- `DecidableEq` is needed since `CDMG.E`, `CDMG.L` are `Finset (Node × Node)`.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
def adjacent (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.sus v1 v2
lean_blocks.explanation: Item i of the LN block: $v_1$ and $v_2$ are *adjacent in $G$* iff some edge of $G$ — directed in either orientation, or bidirected — connects them. The body is a one-line delegation to `G.sus v1 v2`, the *susceptibility* primitive defined in `CDMGNotation.lean`, which already unfolds to the three-way disjunction `(v1, v2) ∈ G.E ∨ (v2, v1) ∈ G.E ∨ s(v1, v2) ∈ G.L`. The `huh`-disjunct uses Mathlib's `Sym2` notation `s(v1, v2)` because `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` is carried by unordered pairs; that detail is absorbed inside `sus` and does not surface here. Symmetry `G.adjacent v1 v2 ↔ G.adjacent v2 v1` is inherited from `sus` — the first two disjuncts swap and the `Sym2` quotient gives `s(v1, v2) = s(v2, v1)` definitionally, so no `hL_symm` invocation is needed. The declaration is a `def`, not an `abbrev`, so it remains a named abstraction at every call site; downstream consumers (`def_3_4` walks, `def_3_5` family relations, chapters 6–7 separations) unfold on demand.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Adjacency in `G`: one-line delegation to `G.sus`, the notation primitive.
-- `G : CDMG Node` is the ambient graph; `v1 v2 : Node` are the vertices being tested.
-- Return type `Prop` keeps this a proposition (not `Bool`); decidability is inherited from `sus`.
def adjacent (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  -- `G.sus v1 v2` unfolds to `tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh`, i.e. some directed or bidirected edge between `v1` and `v2`.
  G.sus v1 v2

lean_blocks.code:
def intoE (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (e : Node × Node) : Prop :=
  e ∈ G.E ∧ e.2 = v
lean_blocks.explanation: Directed-channel (E-channel) half of LN item ii: an ordered pair `e : Node × Node` is an *edge into $v$* on the directed channel iff `e ∈ G.E` and `e.2 = v` (head-at-`v`). The name `intoE` makes the channel explicit; this declaration sits next to `intoL` (L-channel half), since `G.E : Finset (Node × Node)` and `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` have distinct carriers and cannot share a single predicate argument. The `e.2 = v` check pins `v` to the *head* coordinate under the chapter-wide `(tail, head)` directed-edge convention from `CDMGNotation.lean` (`tuh` writes `(v1, v2) ∈ G.E` for `v1 → v2`). No precondition `e ∈ G.E` is threaded through the type — any pair outside `G.E` already falsifies the body's first conjunct. The vertex `v` ranges over the ambient `Node`; `hE_subset` on `CDMG` guarantees `e.2 ∈ G.V`, so a foreign `v` makes the predicate vacuously `False`. Downstream consumers include `def_3_5`'s `Pa^G(v)` (parents) and `def_3_10`'s hard-intervention E-side removal.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `intoE v e`: E-channel "edge into `v`".  Name flags the channel since `intoL` carries the L-half.
-- `e : Node × Node` matches the ordered-pair carrier of `G.E`; no subtype wrap.
def intoE (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (e : Node × Node) : Prop :=
  -- `e ∈ G.E`: directed-channel membership. `e.2 = v`: head coordinate (under the `(tail, head)` convention).
  e ∈ G.E ∧ e.2 = v

lean_blocks.code:
def intoL (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (s : Sym2 Node) : Prop :=
  s ∈ G.L ∧ v ∈ s
lean_blocks.explanation: Bidirected-channel (L-channel) half of LN item ii: an unordered pair `s : Sym2 Node` is an *edge into $v$* on the bidirected channel iff `s ∈ G.L` and `v ∈ s` (i.e. `v` is one of the two endpoints `s` mentions). The argument is `Sym2 Node`, matching `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)`; `Sym2 Node` is Mathlib's symmetric-square quotient `(Node × Node) / swap`, so `s(v1, v2) = s(v2, v1)` definitionally. The membership idiom `v ∈ s` is Mathlib's `Sym2.Mem` / `Membership Node (Sym2 Node)`, which checks both endpoints simultaneously without picking a representative — exactly the LN's "`v` is one endpoint of `s`". This captures the LN-faithful "into both endpoints" reading of a `\huh`-edge: `G.intoL v1 s(v1, v2)` and `G.intoL v2 s(v1, v2)` both hold by construction. No precondition `s ∈ G.L` is threaded; the body discharges it. `v` ranges over ambient `Node` — `hL_subset` keeps any `v ∈ s ∈ G.L` inside `G.V`, so foreign vertices make the predicate vacuously `False`. Downstream consumers: `def_3_5`'s siblings `Sib^G(v)`, `def_3_10`'s L-side removal, `claim_3_22`'s σ-separation symmetry.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `intoL v s`: L-channel "edge into `v`".  Paired with `intoE`; together they cover LN item ii.
-- `s : Sym2 Node` is the unordered-pair carrier of `G.L`; `Sym2` is Mathlib's swap quotient.
def intoL (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (s : Sym2 Node) : Prop :=
  -- `s ∈ G.L`: bidirected-channel membership.  `v ∈ s`: `Sym2.Mem`, checks both endpoints at once.
  s ∈ G.L ∧ v ∈ s

lean_blocks.code:
def outOf (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (e : Node × Node) : Prop :=
  e ∈ G.E ∧ e.1 = v
lean_blocks.explanation: Dual of `into`'s `E`-clause: an ordered pair `e` is *out of* `v` iff it is a directed edge (`e ∈ G.E`) with `v` at the tail (`e.1 = v`). Crucially, there is **no** `L`-clause — bidirected edges contribute nothing to `outOf`, by intentional design and per the LN. This is the second half of the `bidirected_huh_into_both_out_of_neither` subtlety: an $L$-edge incident at `v` is *into `v`* but *not out of `v`*, so `into` and `outOf` are **not** complementary classifications of edges incident at `v`. Downstream code that wants "incident but not into" must not reach for `outOf` as the complement. As with `into`, `v` ranges over the ambient `Node` rather than `G.J ∪ G.V`; `hE_subset` makes the predicate vacuously `False` for foreign vertices, so no membership obligation needs threading. Consumers include `def_3_5`'s children `Ch^G(v)` and `def_3_10`'s hard intervention edge removal.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `outOf v e`: only directed edges (`G.E`) with `v` at the tail.
-- Name uses Lean-idiomatic camelCase (`outOf`) since `out` is reserved-adjacent.
def outOf (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (e : Node × Node) : Prop :=
  -- Mirror of `into`'s `E`-clause: tail-at-`v` (`e.1 = v`) instead of head-at-`v`.
  -- No `L`-clause by design: bidirected edges are "out of neither" endpoint.
  e ∈ G.E ∧ e.1 = v

addition_to_the_LN: [inconsistent_writing_order_enumeration_into_vs_out_of] Throughout the enumeration above, the writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ denote the same edge (and the bidirected writings $v_1 \huh v_2$ and $v_2 \huh v_1$ likewise coincide). Membership of an edge in the categories "into $v$" or "out of $v$" depends only on the underlying element of $E$, not on which of these textual writings is used; item 3's explicit listing of both writings and item 2's single-writing enumeration are therefore equivalent under this identification.
design_choices:
**Encode `adjacent` as a one-line delegation to `sus`, not as a fresh three-disjunct.** Item i of the LN block lists adjacency as `v_1 \sus v_2 \in G`, and `sus` (from `CDMGNotation.lean`) already unfolds to `tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh`, i.e. `(v_1, v_2) \in G.E ∨ (v_2, v_1) \in G.E ∨ s(v_1, v_2) \in G.L`. Re-spelling that body inside `adjacent` was rejected because it would duplicate `sus`'s implementation and force downstream proofs to carry two interchangeable names; delegation keeps adjacency definitionally tied to the notation primitive. Symmetry `G.adjacent v_1 v_2 ↔ G.adjacent v_2 v_1` is inherited from `sus`: the first two disjuncts swap, and the `huh`-disjunct's symmetry is the definitional equality `s(v_1, v_2) = s(v_2, v_1)` from Mathlib's `Sym2` swap quotient — no `hL_symm` invocation is needed.

**Use `def` rather than `abbrev` for all four predicates.** `adjacent`, `intoE`, `intoL`, and `outOf` are named LN-derived relations reused as abstractions for walks (`def_3_4`), family sets (`def_3_5`), hard intervention (`def_3_10`), and d-/$\sigma$-separation (chapters 6–7). An `abbrev` would auto-unfold at every elaboration site, eliminating the named abstraction; downstream proofs that need the body opt in via `unfold`.

**Split LN item ii into channel-specific halves `intoE` and `intoL`.** `G.E : Finset (Node × Node)` and `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` have distinct carriers, so a single `into` body cannot simultaneously be an ordered pair (for the E-clause) and an unordered pair (for the L-clause). Two alternatives were considered: a sum-type unification `e : Node × Node ⊕ Sym2 Node` with a `match` inside the body, and an existential wrapper. Both were rejected because every downstream consumer already knows which channel it is on (walk-step constructors `.forwardE`/`.backwardE`/`.bidir`, family operators `Pa`/`Ch` vs `Sib`, collider classifications) — wrapping then unwrapping the sum at the call site is pure overhead. Splitting at the def site pushes the case-analysis to its natural place.

**Type `intoL`'s edge argument as `Sym2 Node` and use the membership idiom `v ∈ s`.** Mathlib's `Sym2.Mem` (`Membership Node (Sym2 Node)`) checks both endpoints of an unordered pair simultaneously without picking a representative — a representative-free encoding of the LN's "`v` is one endpoint of `s`". Destructuring `s` to `s(v_1, v_2)` and checking `v = v_1 ∨ v = v_2` was rejected because the swap quotient has no canonical representative, and consumers would have to thread `Sym2.lift`/`Sym2.ind` rather than the centralised membership lookup. The LN's "into both endpoints" reading of a `\huh`-edge is then automatic: `G.intoL v_1 s(v_1, v_2)` and `G.intoL v_2 s(v_1, v_2)` both hold by `Sym2.mk_left_mem` / `Sym2.mk_right_mem`.

**Parameterise `intoE`, `intoL`, and `outOf` by a single bound vertex `v`, not by a labelled endpoint pair.** The LN's item ii defines "into $v_1$" and "into $v_2$" separately while item iii defines only "out of $v_1$". A single-vertex parameter collapses that asymmetry into one uniformly-quantified statement and resolves the wording subtlety where "out of $v_2$" was never literally defined. An endpoint-pair encoding was rejected because it would propagate the source-text asymmetry into every consumer.

**Let `v` range over the ambient `Node`, not over `G.J ∪ G.V` (or `G.V`).** Restricting at the type level would force every downstream chain (walks, children, intervention) to thread a membership proof. `hE_subset` and `hL_subset` already guarantee that any edge endpoint sits in the right vertex set, so supplying a foreign `v` makes the predicate vacuously `False` — no side-condition is needed.

**Check `e.2 = v` for `intoE` and `e.1 = v` for `outOf`.** Under the chapter-wide `(tail, head)` directed-edge convention from `CDMGNotation.lean`, `(s, t) ∈ G.E` writes the edge from tail `s` to head `t` (= `s \tuh t` = `t \hut s`). The LN says an E-edge is *into* `v` iff `v` is the head, and *out of* `v` iff `v` is the tail. Replacing `e.2 = v` with the symmetric form `e.1 = v ∨ e.2 = v` would conflate "into" with "incident at" for directed edges, sweeping up tail-incident edges the LN classifies as `outOf`.

**Omit any L-clause from `outOf` by design.** Item iii of the LN enumerates only E-channel writings; bidirected edges are "into both endpoints" but "out of neither" — intrinsic to the notes' semantics of latent confounding. Adding an L-clause for surface symmetry with `intoE` + `intoL` was rejected as a content deviation. The consequence is registered: "into $v$" and "out of $v$" are *not* complementary classifications of edges incident at $v$. Any downstream predicate that wants "incident at $v$ but not into $v$" must combine `intoE`/`intoL` with its own incidence predicate, never read off `¬ outOf`.

**Drop any precondition `e ∈ G.E` or `s ∈ G.L` on the predicate arguments.** Threading membership through the type would burden every call site with an obligation the predicate body discharges anyway: any pair outside the relevant carrier already falsifies the body's first conjunct. The LN's "in particular, only ordered pairs $e \in E \cup L$ can be edges into $v$" clause is a consequence, not a hypothesis.
